coredata.dc:title: Stability improvement of N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET based flexible electrochromic device for smart window application
coredata.dc:creator: Ahmad, Khursheed
Shinde, Mahesh A.
Song, Gihwan
Kim, Haekyoung
coredata.dc:description:
The design and development of cost-effective and highly stable flexible transparent conductive electrodes (f-TCEs) is crucial for various optoelectronic applications. Herein, we report the fabrication of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (N-GQD)-protected silver nanowire (AgNWs)-modified polyethylene terephthalate (PET) electrodes as f-TCEs (N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET). Furthermore, we fabricated flexible electrochromic devices (ECDs) for smart window applications using N-GQD/AgNW/PET as the f-TCE. The presence of N-GQDs on the AgNW/PET electrode improved the stability of the developed N-GQD/AgNW/PET-based flexible ECDs. The N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET-based flexible ECDs demonstrated an excellent coloration efficiency (ȠCE) of 42.7 cm2C −
1 with an optical contrast of 45.4 %. Additionally, they demonstrated fast coloring and bleaching times of 6.9, and 5.7 s, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-GQDs
Silver nanowires
Passivation layer
Flexible electrochromic devices
And transparent conductive electrode

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: General characterization of WO3 and N-GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern (a), Raman spectrum (b), and EDX spectrum (c) of N-GQDs. XPS survey scan (d), N1s (e), and C1s (f) scan of N-GQDs.
XRD pattern (a), SEM image (b), and EDX spectrum (c) of WO3. EDX mapping images of W (d), O (e) elements, and high resolution XPS scan of W4f (f), and O1s (g) of WO3.
Sheet resistance (a) and transmittance (b) of different f-TCEs.
SEM image of AgNWs (a), and N-GQDs/AgNWs (b) on PET substrate.
Electron image (a), EDX spectrum (b), and EDX mapping images of N-GQDs/AgNWs (c–f).
Electron image (a), EDX spectrum (b), and EDX mapping images of WO3/N-GQDs/AgNWs (c–f).
Variation in resistance of AgNWs/PET and N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET under IR (a), UV (b), and visible (c) light.
SEM image of AgNWs/PET after exposed to IR (a), UV (b), and visible (c) light. SEM image of N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET after exposed to IR (d), UV (e), and visible (f) light.

Transmittance (%) versus time (s) curves of the AgNWs/PET and N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET-based ECDs (a, b). Optical density versus charge-density curve (c) of N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET-based ECDs. Transmittance of N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET-based ECDs at different applied voltage (d).
Transmittance (%) of the bleaching and coloring states of N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET-based ECDs (a). Bleaching and coloring states of N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET-based ECDs (b).
Comparison of the ȠCE, △T, coloring time, and bleaching time of the N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET based ECDs with previous reports [19,21,27–30].

head.ce:title: Stability improvement of N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET based flexible electrochromic device for smart window application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, and Organic and Carbon Electronics Laboratories (ORaCEL), North Carolina State University, Raleigh, NC 27695, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, and Organic and Carbon Electronics Laboratories (ORaCEL)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, and Organic and Carbon Electronics Laboratories (ORaCEL), North Carolina State University, Raleigh, NC 27695, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The design and development of cost-effective and highly stable flexible transparent conductive electrodes (f-TCEs) is crucial for various optoelectronic applications. Herein, we report the fabrication of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot (N-GQD)-protected silver nanowire (AgNWs)-modified polyethylene terephthalate (PET) electrodes as f-TCEs (N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET). Furthermore, we fabricated flexible electrochromic devices (ECDs) for smart window applications using N-GQD/AgNW/PET as the f-TCE. The presence of N-GQDs on the AgNW/PET electrode improved the stability of the developed N-GQD/AgNW/PET-based flexible ECDs. The N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET-based flexible ECDs demonstrated an excellent coloration efficiency (ȠCE) of 42.7 cm2C −
1 with an optical contrast of 45.4 %. Additionally, they demonstrated fast coloring and bleaching times of 6.9, and 5.7 s, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, electrochromic devices (ECDs) have attracted significant interest [1]. ECDs can be implanted as smart windows that have the potential to save energy by controlling and maintaining the temperature in buildings and indoor spaces [2,3]. ECDs are based on the principle of electrochromism and consisted of a TCE, an electrochromic layer, and an electrolyte [4]. TCE plays a crucial role in the fabrication of ECDs [5]. In previous years, indium tin oxide (ITO), fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO), and various rigid substrates have been used as TCEs for the development of ECDs [6–8]. FTO and ITO, which are rigid substrate-based TCEs, suffer from poor mechanical and chemical stabilities [9]. Moreover, the brittleness of ITO- and FTO-based TCEs inhibit their potential application in stretchable, flexible, and wearable electronic or optoelectronic devices, such as sensors, electronic papers, e-skins, antiglare mirrors, and foldable smart windows [10]. Thus, the design and fabrication of flexible transparent conductive electrodes (f-TCEs) have received immense interest from scientific scholars because of their significant applications in ECDs, smart windows, solar cells, and photodetectors [11]. Various carbon-based materials such as carbon nanotubes and reduced graphene oxide, conducting polymers, nanostructured materials, and silver nanowires (AgNWs) have been used as flexible TCE materials [12–14]. Reported literature suggests that AgNWs are the most significant and efficient electrode materials for the construction of f-TCEs with improved optical, mechanical, electrical, flexible, and thermal properties [15]. AgNWs can be oxidized under an applied potential in the presence of an electrolyte [16]. This can significantly reduce the bleaching and coloring states of the fabricated flexible ECDs [17]. Thus, some passivation layers are required to protect the AgNWs. Poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) polystyrene sulfonate (PEDOT:PSS) was used as a protective layer to reduce AgNW oxidation [18]. In another study, rGO was used to protect the AgNWs [19]. Ahmad et al. [20] reported molybdenum disulfide (MoS2)/rGO composite as protection layer whereas Mallikarjuna et al. [21] applied MoS2 as passivation layer to protect the AgNWs. To develop high-performance flexible ECDs, the passivation layer should exhibit excellent stability and conductivity.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) consist of few layers of graphene atoms with size less than 30 nm. The GQDs possess excellent electrical conductivity, larger diameter, high surface area, and excellent surface grafting via conjugated π-π network [22]. GQDs are zero-dimensional materials, and the presence of edge atoms in the GQDs may provide strong interactions with surrounding molecules compared to the graphene or graphene oxide [23]. The physiochemical properties of the GQDs can be further enhanced by introducing heteroatoms. Incorporation or doping of GQDs with heteroatoms can significantly improve the intrinsic and electronic properties of the GQDs [24]. A nitrogen (N) atom with five valence electrons is a suitable candidate for the doping of GQDs [25].

In this study, we prepared N-doped GQDs (N-GQDs) using a hydrothermal method and employed them as a passivation layer for the development of flexible ECDs. To the best of our knowledge, there are no reports on the use of N-GQDs as protective or passivation layers to protect AgNWs. In this study, we fabricated flexible ECDs using N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET as f-TCEs, which exhibited excellent stability and performance.

Tungsten powder (W) was obtained from Alfa Aesar, and hydrogen peroxide (30 wt % in H2O and H2O2) was purchased from Duksan, South Korea. Citric acid, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and urea were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich. EL-72 cells were obtained from Adcro. All other chemicals, solvents, and reagents were purchased from Merck, Sigma–Aldrich, Alfa Aesar, and Fisher Scientific. AgNWs solution (length = 25 µm and diameter = 25 nm) was purchased from SG Flexio.

The formation and phase purity of the N-GQD and WO3 samples were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD). Scanning electron microscopy picture of WO3 were obtained using a Hitachi S-4800 microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific) was used to characterize the binding energies and chemical states of the N-GQDs and WO3 samples. The electrochromic and optical properties of the fabricated ECDs and f-TCE, respectively, were investigated using ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrometry (Thermo Scientific; GENESYS™ 10 s) at an excitation wavelength of 700 nm. A potentiostat voltage supplier (Weis 500 WonATech) was used to collect data using Visionlite software.

N-GQDs were prepared using citric acid and urea as the carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively [23]. 0.09 g urea and 0.5 g and citric acid were dissolved in distilled water and transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated at 200 °C for 8 h and naturally cooled to an ambient temperature. The obtained dispersion was filtered by 0.45 µm filter, which removed the large particles. The PH of the solution was adjusted to 7 using NaOH. The N-GQD solution was diluted to 100 mL and stored at 4 °C.

WO3 was prepared using a previously reported method [26]. 6.52 g of the W powder was mixed with 93.2 g of hydrogen peroxide (31 wt % in water) and stirred at room temperature. The reaction mixture was heated at 100 °C to evaporate the solvent. The final product was a pale yellow powder. WO3 (30 wt %) was dispersed in a solution of distilled water and isopropanol (the ratio of distilled water to isopropanol was 50:50) using ultra-sonication for 6 h, to yield a homogeneous WO3 ink.

To fabricate flexible ECDs, we used N-GQD/AgNW/PET or AgNW/PET as f-TCEs. PET was adopted as a flexible electrode substrate, which was modified with AgNWs using a bar-coating approach (Mayer rod was used to prepare the AGNWs on PET substrate). Furthermore, AgNW-modified PET (AgNW/PET) was allowed to dry at room temperature for a few minutes, followed by heating at 90–100 °C for 10 min. Furthermore, N-GQDs were spin-coated onto the modified AgNW/PET electrode at a spin speed of 4000 rpm for 25 s under ambient conditions. The electrode was heated at 90–100 °C for 10 min in a vacuum oven.

The flexible ECDs were prepared at room temperature under aerobic conditions. WO3 ink (30 wt % in distilled water and isopropanol) was spin coated onto N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET electrode at a spin speed of 5000 rpm for 25 sec and dried in a vacuum oven for 8–10 h at 60 °C. A gel electrolyte was prepared by dissolving 10 wt % ferrocene in EL-72 and coating it on the other fabricated N-GQD/AgNW/PET electrode using the drop-cast method. Further, WO3/N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET and EL-72/N-GQD/AgNW/PET electrodes were assembled and laminated to construct flexible ECDs. Flexible ECDs without N-GQDs were also developed to investigate the role of N-GQD as a passivation layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General characterization of WO3 and N-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized N-GQDs and WO3 were characterized by XRD (Instrument: X’pert Pro). The obtained X-ray diffractograms of the N-GQDs and WO3 have been displayed in Fig. 1 a and 2a, respectively. The XRD pattern of the N-GQDs exhibited a broad diffraction peak at ∼25.55°, which corresponds to the (002) diffraction plane, and this is consistent with a previous study [23]. This confirms the formation of N-GQDs. XRD investigations of the WO3 exhibits the presence of well-defined (002), (200), and (202) diffraction planes, as shown in Fig. 2 a. The synthesized WO3 had a monoclinic phase with a good crystalline nature. The obtained X-ray diffractogram of WO3 was well-matched with previously reported JCPDS card number 01-83-0950. The XRD patterns of N-GQDs (Fig. 1a) and WO3 (Fig. 2a) did not show any other diffraction peaks for impurities, indicating the successful formation of N-GQDs and WO3 with good phase purity. A transmission electron microscopic (TEM) image of the N-GQDs has been presented in Fig. S1. The Raman spectra of the N-GQDs has been presented in Fig. 1b. The observations revealed that the N-GQDs possessed a graphitic nature along with defects. Furthermore, EDX spectrum of the N-GQDs was also obtained to confirm the presence of N atoms in the prepared N-GQDs. The obtained EDX spectrum of N-GQDs has been displayed in Fig. 1c which exhibited the EDX peaks for N, C, and O elements. Thus, it is suggested that N-GQDs has been formed. Subsequently, XPS (Thermo Scientific) technique was used to determine the oxidation/chemical states and elemental compositions of the N-GQD and WO3 samples. An XPS survey scan of the N-GQDs, indicating the presence of N, C, and O, is shown in Fig. 1d. This suggests the successful formation of N-GQDs (Fig. 1d). Fig. 1e shows the N1s spectrum of N-GQDs. The N1s spectrum showed three deconvoluted peaks, which could be assigned to graphitic N, pyridinic N, and pyrrolic N (Fig. 1e). For case of C1s, five peaks were observed, which were assigned to CC, (CC, CH, NC), COH, (COC, NC), and CO. All XPS investigations revealed the successful formation of N-GQDs (Fig. 1f) [25]. Fig. 2b shows a SEM (Hitachi; S-4800) image of the prepared WO3 thin film and suggests a uniform distribution of WO3 nanoparticles. The EDX spectrum of WO3 has been presented in Fig. 2c which exhibited the presence of EDX peaks for W, and O elements and confirmed the decent phase purity of the prepared WO3. The EDX mapping image for W and O elements are presented in Fig. 2d and 2e, respectively. Fig. 2f shows a high-resolution W4f scan of WO3, which indicates the presence of W6+ and W5+ states. The W6+ and W5+ states are responsible for the bleaching and coloring states of ECDs, which involve the intervalence charge transfer process [3]. Fig. 2g shows the O1s of WO3 which contains two peaks. These two peaks can be assigned to the WO and WOH bonds.

The transparency and sheet resistance of the f-TCE play crucial roles in achieving high coloration efficiency, optical contrast, and fast toggling. Thus, f-TCEs should have high transparency and low sheet resistance to develop high-performance flexible ECDs for smart-window applications. Therefore, we optimized the transparency and sheet resistance of N-GQD/AgNW/PET electrodes. Fig. 3 a shows the sheet resistances of the AgNW/PET and N-GQD/AgNW/PET electrodes. The AgNWs/PET exhibits a lower sheet-resistance of 21.47 Ω/sq, whereas N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET (for single layer) has sheet-resistance of 19.43 Ω/sq. This decrease in the sheet resistance of N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET (for single layer) may be attributed to the introduction of N-GQDs which covers the space between the AgNWs. In other case, triple-layer N-GQD/AgNW/PET exhibited a sheet sheet-resistance of 39.45 Ω/sq. This showed that sheet resistance of the N-GQD/AgNW/PET electrode increased with an increase in the number of N-GQDs layers on the AgNW/PET substrate. This behavior may be due to the lower conductivity of N-GQDs compared to the AgNWs. The UV–vis transmittance spectra of the PET, AgNWs/PET and N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET (single, and triple layers) are shown in Fig. 3b. The results indicated that the PET has the highest transparency (94.85 % at 700 nm) while AgNWs coated PET (AgNWs/PET) possess reduced transparency (91.90 % at 700 nm). The N-GQD/AgNW/PET with single layer showed reasonable transparency (89.33 % at 700 nm). On the other side, N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET with a triple layer exhibited lowest transparency (86.02 % at 700 nm) compared to the other f-TCEs (Fig. 3b). Therefore, we selected the N-GQD/AgNW/PET (single-layer) as the final optimized f-TCE for the construction of flexible ECDs. The surface morphological properties of the AgNW/PET and N-GQD/AgNW/PET were evaluated using SEM. SEM images of AgNW/PET and N-GQD/AgNW/PET are presented in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4a shows the presence of AgNWs on the PET substrates, whereas Fig. 4b exhibit the presence of N-GQDs on the AgNW/PET substrate. To further confirm the presence of N-GQDs on the AgNW/PET surface, EDX analysis was carried out. Fig. 5 a shows the electron image of the N-GQDs/AgNWs whereas Fig. 5b displays the EDX spectrum of the fabricated N-GQDs/AgNWs. The EDX spectrum exhibited the presence of C, N, O, and Ag elements (Fig. 5b). The EDX mapping images of Ag, N, C, and O elements are presented in Fig. 5c, Fig. 5d, Fig. 5e and Fig. 5f, respectively. The EDX mapping images indicate the presence of C, N, Ag, and O, which are related to the presence of N-GQDs on the AgNW/PET surface. The EDX spectrum of AgNWs has been presented in Fig. S2. The EDX spectrum showed the presence of Ag only. There was no peak for C, or N elements detected (Fig. S2). The EDX analysis for WO3/N-GQDs/AgNWs was also carried out. The electron image and EDX spectrum of the WO3/N-GQDs/AgNWs are provided in Fig. 6 a and 6b, respectively. The obtained results exhibited the presence of C, N, O, W, and Ag elements (Fig. 6b). The EDX mapping images of the Ag, C, N, W and O elements are provided in Fig. 6c, Fig. 6d, Fig. 6e and Fig. 6f, respectively. The stabilities of the AgNW/PET and N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET (width = 3 cm and length = 4 cm) under infrared (IR), ultraviolet (UV), and visible light exposure were examined. To check the stability of the AgNW/PET and N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET, the electrodes were connected to a multimeter using copper tape and exposed to different lights. The changes in the resistances of the AgNW/PET and N-GQD/AgNW/PET were calculated as Rt-Ro/Rt. Here, Ro is the initial resistance and Rt is the resistance of the AgNW/PET or N-GQD/AgNW/PET as a function of time. Fig. 7 a shows the decrease in the resistance of AgNW/PET due to nanowire welding induced by photonic energy under IR light exposure. The resistance of the AgNW/PET further increased and failed after 60 min (Fig. 7a) under IR light exposure. Under UV exposure, the change in the resistance of the AgNW/PET increased for 90 h (Fig. 7b). Similarly, the change in the resistance of the AgNW/PET increased for 100 h under visible light exposure (Fig. 7c). Thus, the above observations suggest that continuous light exposure plays a crucial role in accelerating the oxidation or photocorrosion of the AgNWs, which damages the AgNW junctions. This indicates the poor stability of the AgNWs. In contrast, N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET exhibited excellent stability under IR (Fig. 7a), UV (Fig. 7b), and visible (Fig. 7c) light exposure at similar conditions. SEM images of the AgNW/PET after exposure to IR, UV, and visible light are presented in Fig. 8 a, Fig. 8b, and Fig. 8c, respectively. The SEM results also show that the AgNWs were damaged after exposure to IR, UV, and visible light. In contrast, the SEM images of the N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET exhibited excellent stability under IR, UV, and visible light exposure, as shown in Fig. 8b, Fig. 8d, and Fig. 8e, respectively. This revealed that presence of N-GQDs improve the stability of the AgNWs. N-GQDs possess good surface area which may form protective barriers on the surface of AgNWs. The N-GQDs can block the direct contact of the AgNWs with oxygen, light or moisture and improve the stability of AgNWs.

AgNW/PET- and N-GQD/AgNW/PET-based ECDs were fabricated under similar conditions. The electrochromic performances of the AgNW/PET- and N-GQD/AgNW/PET-based ECDs were evaluated in terms of transmittance. Transmittance (%) against time (s) curves were recorded in the applied potential range of 0 V (bleached state) to −1 V (colored state). The recorded transmittance (%) versus time (s) graphs of the AgNW/PET- and N-GQD/AgNW/PET-based ECDs are shown in Fig. 9 a. The transmittance (%) versus time (s) graphs of the AgNWs/PET and N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET-based ECDs (at 700 nm wavelength) exhibited optical contrasts (△T) of 53.4 and 45.4 %, respectively. The transmittance (%) versus time (s) graphs of the AgNW/PET-based ECDs show that the AgNW/PET-based ECDs were damaged after 1st cycle probably because of the oxidation of the AgNWs. In contrast, the transmittance (%) versus time (s) graphs of the N-GQD/AgNW/PET-based ECDs exhibited good cyclic stability up to five cycles (Fig. 9b). This indicated that the N-GQDs successfully protected the AgNWs and acted as passivation layers. The electrochromic behavior and performance of the N-GQD/AgNW/PET-based ECDs were evaluated by calculating the coloration efficiency (ȠCE) and switching times (coloring and bleaching times). The ȠCE of the N-GQD/AgNW/PET-based ECDs was calculated using Equations (1) and (2).
(1)
η
CE
= Δ O D / Δ O Q

Here, △OD = change in optical density, and Q = charge density.
(2)
Δ O D = l o g (
T b
/
T c
)
Here, Tc is the transmittance in the colored state, and Tb is the transmittance in the bleached state.

The N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET-based ECDs showed △T of 45.4 % with ȠCE of 42.7 cm2C −
1. The N-GQD/Ag NW/PET-based ECDs exhibited bleaching and coloring times of 5.7 and 6.9 s, respectively. The calculated ȠCE, △T, coloring time, and bleaching time of the N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET-based ECDs are summarized in Table 1 . The △OD versus charge density curve of the N-GQD/Ag NW/PET-based ECDs is shown in Fig. 9c. The transmittances of the N-GQD/AgNW/PET-based ECDs at different voltages are shown in Fig. 9d. The transmittance (%) versus wavelength graphs of the bleaching and coloring states of the N-GQD/AgNW/PET-based ECDs are shown in Fig. 10 a. The observations show a good optical contrast of 45.4 % at a wavelength of 700 nm. The colored and bleached states of the fabricated N-GQDs/AgNWs/PET-based ECDs have been illustrated in Fig. 10b. For practical applications, ECDs should exhibit good cycling stability. Therefore, we examined the cyclic stability of the N-GQD/AgNW/PET-based ECDs. The transmittance (%) versus time (s) of the N-GQD/Ag NW/PET-based ECDs is shown in Fig. 11 . These observations suggest that N-GQD/AgNW/PET-based ECDs possess good stability up to 500 cycles. This indicates that the presence of N-GQDs improves the stability of the N-GQD/AgNW/PET-based ECDs. Bending stability is one of the most important features of flexible ECDs for smart-window applications. Hence, we examined the bending stability of N-GQD/AgNW/PET-based ECDs up to 1200 bending cycles. The Transmittance (%) versus time (s) graphs for the 1st, 200th, 500th, 800th, 1000th, and 1200th bending cycles are shown in Fig. S3. The results show that the N-GQD/AgNW/PET-based ECDs retained good stability up to the 1200th bending cycle. Our results are comparable to those of previous studies, as shown in Table 1.

In this study, N-GQDs were successfully prepared using a hydrothermal method. The synthesized N-GQDs were characterized by XRD, EDS, and XPS. Furthermore, AgNWs were deposited on a PET substrate using a bar coating method. N-GQDs were further applied as passivation layers to protect the AgNWs. The fabricated N-GQD/AgNW/PET was used as the flexible electrode for the fabrication of flexible ECDs. The N-GQD/AgNW/PET-based flexible ECDs exhibited decent optical contrast and coloration efficiency. Additionally, the fabricated ECDs exhibited excellent cycling and bending stabilities.

Khursheed Ahmad: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Mahesh A. Shinde: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Gihwan Song: Methodology, Formal analysis. Haekyoung Kim: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Korea Innovation Foundation (INNOPOLIS) [grant number 2020-DD-UP-0278] and National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korean government (MSIT) NRF-2022R1A2C1005585).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2023.111383.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sol-gel fabrication of NiO and NiO/WO3 based electrochromic device on ITO and flexible substrate
Ceramic. Int.

Adv. Opt. Mater.

Ceramic. Int.

Energy Technol.

ACS Nano

sb:title.sb:maintitle: This review will look at each chromogenic technology, its characteristics, and potential applications set in the context of other competitive switchable technologies. It will cover the applications and market potential for a variety of media including mir
Mater. Today

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.M. Lampert, This review will look at each chromogenic technology, its characteristics, and potential applications set in the context of other competitive switchable technologies. It will cover the applications and market potential for a variety of media including mir, Mater. Today 7 (2004) 28–35.
